reasoning: The informal statement assumes $f$ is "infinitely differentiable" ($C^\infty$). In the Lean formalization, `ContDiff ‚Ñù ‚ä§ f` with Mathlib v4.27.0 uses the new signature `ContDiff (n : WithTop ‚Ñï‚àû)`. Here `‚ä§ : WithTop ‚Ñï‚àû` is the analytic top (scoped notation `œâ`), not the $C^\infty$ top (which would be `‚àû = ((‚ä§ : ‚Ñï‚àû) : WithTop ‚Ñï‚àû)`). This is confirmed by `contDiff_omega_iff_analyticOnNhd : ContDiff ùïú œâ f ‚Üî AnalyticOnNhd ùïú f univ` in `Mathlib/Analysis/Calculus/ContDiff/Defs.lean`. Thus the Lean hypothesis requires $f$ to be real-analytic on $\mathbb{R}$, which is strictly stronger than $C^\infty$ (e.g., $e^{-1/x^2}$ is $C^\infty$ but not analytic at 0). The informal claim ranges over all $C^\infty$ functions satisfying the interpolation condition, while the Lean version only covers the narrower class of real-analytic ones, so the Lean formalization is logically implied by ‚Äî but does not imply ‚Äî the informal statement.
